rulings.Case Title: GEORGIA-PACIFIC CORRUGATED III LLC VS. TAN PACKAGING LLC et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn Iso Mtn To Impose Issue And Terminating Sanctions; To Compel Responsse To Production Of Documents; To Deem All Request For Admission Admitted; To Deem All Objections Waived; And For Monetary Sanctions In The Amount Of $1067.50
rulings.Rulings: Hearing set for Friday, June 7, 2013, Line 3. Defendant JOSEPH TULLEY'S Motion to Impose Issue and Terminating Sanctions; to Compel Responsse to Production of Documents; to Deem All Request for Admission Admitted; to Deem All Objections Waived; and for Monetary Sanctions in the Amount of $1067.50 Pro Tem Judge Peter Van Zandt, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Motion is GRANTED in part and DENIED in part. Defendants have provided verified responses to the Request for Production and have satisfied their burden. Defendant failed to provide verified responses to the Request for Admissions. The party to which requests for admissions are directed must sign the response under oath. (CCP §2033.240(a)) If the party responds to requests for admission but those responses are unverified, they are "tantamount to no responses at all," and the proponent is entitled to obtain an order that the requests are deemed admitted. (Appleton v. Superior Court (1988) 206 CA3d 632, 636) Here, Defendant failed to respond to requests for admission and then when compelled by the Court to do so, served unverified responses. Defendant's Reply brief sums it up well, the unverified responses "¿were sent 2 weeks after Mr. Tulley filed this motion; they were sent 68 days after the Court ordered the Plaintiff to comply; and they were sent 6 months after Mr. Tulley sent the discovery requests to Plaintiff¿"(Reply to Opposition, 2:20-23) Defendant's Request for Admission 1, 2, and 3 are DEEMED ADMITTED. The Court DENIES Defendant's request for a "terminating sanction." Defendant's request for sanctions in the form of attorneys fees and costs was unopposed. Sanctions in the amount of $1,067.50 are awarded to Defendant Joseph Tulley and his counsel Jeffrey B. Workman to be paid by Plaintiff Georgia-Pacific within 10 days of service of the Notice of Entry of Order on this motion. Any party who contests this tentative ruling must send an email to peter.vanzandt@leclairryan.com or a fax to 415.391.8766 with a copy to all other parties by 4pm one day prior to the hearing stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem Judge hearing the motion and the Pro Tem Judge will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
rulings.Judge: Judge Pro Tem: Peter Van Zandt

rulings.Case Title: EDDIE VALADEZ VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR LEAVE TO To Submit Tardy Expert Witness Infor
rulings.Rulings: Hearing set for Friday, June 7, 2013, Line 6. Defendant CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S Motion for Leave to Submit Tardy Expert Witness Information Pro Tem Judge Peter Van Zandt, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: This motion seeks two forms of relief. The first is seeking leave to submit tardy expert witness information and the second is to compel a mental examination of the Plaintiff. As to the first relief sought by Defendant CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, seeking leave to submit tardy expert witness information, the motion is GRANTED. Good cause exists pursuant to CCP §2034.720. Plaintiff's counsel was aware of Dr. Berg well in advance of the disclosure deadline, as a result, the Court does not find that Plaintiff suffered any prejudice related to the tardy formal disclosure. Defendant declares that a calendaring mistake was made and was mitigated by service of the disclosure by facsimile which the Plaintiff admits receiving. Defendant has sought leave from the Court to remedy the late disclosure. As to the second relief sought by Defendant CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO to compel the mental examination of Plaintiff, the motion is GRANTED. Good cause exists under the factors set forth in Vinson v. Superior Court (1987) 43 C.3d 833, 840. Plaintiff has put his mental condition in controversy. Plaintiff's counsel even offered a stipulation to the mental examination with Dr. Berg which indicates agreement that Plaintiff's mental condition is in controversy. The parties are ordered to meet and confer in advance of the hearing and select agreed upon dates for the examination of Plaintiff, the date Dr. Berg's report will be available and date of Dr. Berg's deposition. With a trial date of July 1, 2013, the deposition of Dr. Berg should take place no later than June 26, 2013 unless stipulated otherwise by the parties. Any party who contests this tentative ruling must send an email to peter.vanzandt@leclairryan.com or a fax to 415.391.8766 with a copy to all other parties by 4pm one day prior to the hearing stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem Judge hearing the motion and the Pro Tem Judge will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
rulings.Judge: Judge Pro Tem: Peter Van Zandt

The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: ERIC CLEVELAND et al VS. FRANK MATEO III et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Eric Cleveland'S Form Interrogatories, Set One, Special Interrogatories Set One; Request For Production Of Documents, Set One And Request For Monetary Sanctions Agaainst Deft In The Amount Of $1940
rulings.Rulings: Hearing set for Friday, June 7, 2013, Line 5. Plaintiffs ERIC CLEVELAND and WENDY MORTENSON Motion to Compel Responses to Eric Cleveland's Form Interrogatories, Set One, Special Interrogatories Set One; Request For Production of Documents, Set One and Request for Monetary Sanctions Against Defendant in the Amount of $1940 Pro Tem Judge Peter Van Zandt, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: GRANTED. Defendant FRANK MATEO, III and his new counsel MELISSA WOOD EISENBERG who was substituted for P. Richard Colombatto on June 4, 2013 are ORDERED to provide complete, verified responses to Plaintiff ERIC CLEVELAND's Form Interrogatories, Set One, Special Interrogatories, Set One and Request for Production of Documents, Set One within 20 calendar days of the service of the Notice of Entry of Order on the motion. Plaintiff's request for sanctions is DENIED. Any party who contests this tentative ruling must send an email to peter.vanzandt@leclairryan.com or a fax to 415.391.8766 with a copy to all other parties by 4pm one day prior to the hearing stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem Judge hearing the motion and the Pro Tem Judge will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
rulings.Judge: Judge Pro Tem: Peter Van Zandt
